top_items.title: F1 Standings
top_items.summary: Drivers: 1. Andrea Kimi Antonelli (156) | 2. Lewis Hamilton (115) | 3. George Russell (106). Constructors: 1. Mercedes (262) | 2. Ferrari (190) | 3. McLaren (141). Next GP: Austrian Grand Prix (2026-06-28 13:00:00Z). Last GP winner: Lewis Hamilton (Ferrari) at Barcelona Grand Prix.
top_items.body:
Drivers Championship Top 3: 1. Andrea Kimi Antonelli (156) | 2. Lewis Hamilton (115) | 3. George Russell (106)
Constructors Championship Top 3: 1. Mercedes (262) | 2. Ferrari (190) | 3. McLaren (141)
Next GP: Austrian Grand Prix on 2026-06-28 13:00:00Z
Last GP: Barcelona Grand Prix winner Lewis Hamilton (Ferrari)

highlights: After surviving an early-stage battle against Ferrari's Charles Leclerc, polesitter George Russell led teammate Kimi Antonelli to Mercedes's 61st 1–2 finish.
Leclerc rounded out the podium for Ferrari ahead of teammate Lewis Hamilton; both drivers led for at least one lap during the Grand Prix.

His teammate, 19-year-old Kimi Antonelli, became the youngest Formula One polesitter, second-youngest Grand Prix winner and the first Italian to win a Grand Prix since Giancarlo Fisichella at the 2006 Malaysian Grand Prix.
Antonelli led a Mercedes 1–2 ahead of Russell and Lewis Hamilton, who achieved his maiden podium with Ferrari.
Antonelli took the lead of the Drivers' Championship for the first time in his career, becoming the youngest World Drivers' Championship leader at 19 years and 216 days.
Doing this, he also became Italy's first Drivers' Championship leader since Giancarlo Fisichella led the 2005 championship.
The McLarens of Norris and Piastri completed the podium, making McLaren's second podium of the 2026 season and first double podium.

George Russell (Mercedes) converted pole position for the sprint event to a victory, following a short battle for the lead with Kimi Antonelli, Lando Norris (McLaren) finished second with Antonelli third.
Russell then took pole position for the main race, where he and Antonelli again battled in the opening stages before a battery failure led to his first retirement since the 2024 British Grand Prix.
Antonelli inherited the lead and the race win, taking his fourth consecutive victory.

The podium was completed by Lewis Hamilton (Ferrari) and Max Verstappen (Red Bull), the latter taking his and Red Bull's first podium of the season, and Ford's first podium since Jordan's victory at the 2003 Brazilian Grand Prix.
The current championship leader had the new simulator installed ahead of his win at the Canadian Grand Prix, the fourth of his five consecutive victories so far this season.
Following a string of retirements for Mercedes, including those of George Russell at the Canadian Grand Prix and Kimi Antonelli at the ...
It was held on 7 June 2026, at the Circuit de Monaco in Monaco, and was the sixth round of the 2026 Formula One World Championship.
Converting pole position into a victory, Kimi Antonelli (Mercedes) became the youngest driver to win the Monaco Grand Prix and the youngest to achieve a Grand Slam.

Lewis Hamilton (Ferrari) and Pierre Gasly (Alpine) completed the podium, the latter obtaining his first podium since the 2024 São Paulo Grand Prix, after his two time penalties were overturned following a right to review.
Gasly “completely understands” rivals’ sense of “injustice” over his cancelled penalty | Formula 1.

items.body:
Drivers Championship Top 3: 1. Andrea Kimi Antonelli (156) | 2. Lewis Hamilton (115) | 3. George Russell (106)

highlights: After surviving an early-stage battle against Ferrari's Charles Leclerc, polesitter George Russell led teammate Kimi Antonelli to Mercedes's 61st 1–2 finish.
Leclerc rounded out the podium for Ferrari ahead of teammate Lewis Hamilton; both drivers led for at least one lap during the Grand Prix.

His teammate, 19-year-old Kimi Antonelli, became the youngest Formula One polesitter, second-youngest Grand Prix winner and the first Italian to win a Grand Prix since Giancarlo Fisichella at the 2006 Malaysian Grand Prix.
Antonelli led a Mercedes 1–2 ahead of Russell and Lewis Hamilton, who achieved his maiden podium with Ferrari.
Antonelli took the lead of the Drivers' Championship for the first time in his career, becoming the youngest World Drivers' Championship leader at 19 years and 216 days.
Doing this, he also became Italy's first Drivers' Championship leader since Giancarlo Fisichella led the 2005 championship.
The McLarens of Norris and Piastri completed the podium, making McLaren's second podium of the 2026 season and first double podium.

George Russell (Mercedes) converted pole position for the sprint event to a victory, following a short battle for the lead with Kimi Antonelli, Lando Norris (McLaren) finished second with Antonelli third.
Russell then took pole position for the main race, where he and Antonelli again battled in the opening stages before a battery failure led to his first retirement since the 2024 British Grand Prix.
Antonelli inherited the lead and the race win, taking his fourth consecutive victory.

The podium was completed by Lewis Hamilton (Ferrari) and Max Verstappen (Red Bull), the latter taking his and Red Bull's first podium of the season, and Ford's first podium since Jordan's victory at the 2003 Brazilian Grand Prix.
The current championship leader had the new simulator installed ahead of his win at the Canadian Grand Prix, the fourth of his five consecutive victories so far this season.

Lewis Hamilton (Ferrari) and Pierre Gasly (Alpine) completed the podium, the latter obtaining his first podium since the 2024 São Paulo Grand Prix, after his two time penalties were overturned following a right to review.

items.title: Gulf shares concerns with US as Iran’s influence and power continue by proxy
items.summary: Analysts and western leaders believe Iran will increase support of militia groups despite deal between US and Tehran As Marco Rubio ended his brief visit to the Middle East on Friday, he sought to cast in the best possible light his discussions with leaders of the Gulf states. Those leaders are deeply anxious that the deal agreed earlier this month between Iran and the US fails to address their worries about continued Iranian efforts to project power and influence throughout the region. “They’ve shared with us some very concrete concerns,” the US secretary of state admitted, and insisted that any definitive agreement will require Tehran to not only restrict its nuclear programme but also halt its support of Hamas in Gaza, Hezbollah in Lebanon, militia in Iraq and the Houthis in Yemen. Continue reading...
items.body: Analysts and western leaders believe Iran will increase support of militia groups despite deal between US and Tehran As Marco Rubio ended his brief visit to the Middle East on Friday, he sought to cast in the best possible light his discussions with leaders of the Gulf states. Those leaders are deeply anxious that the deal agreed earlier this month between Iran and the US fails to address their worries about continued Iranian efforts to project power and influence throughout the region. “They’ve shared with us some very concrete concerns,” the US secretary of state admitted, and insisted that any definitive agreement will require Tehran to not only restrict its nuclear programme but also halt its support of Hamas in Gaza, Hezbollah in Lebanon, militia in Iraq and the Houthis in Yemen. Continue reading...

items.title: Greens examining how party decides policy as membership triples under Zack Polanski
items.summary: Exclusive: Concerns in-person vote system is giving bigger say to organised fringe activists who can attend conference Senior Greens are examining ways to revamp the party’s structures to make it more effective and representative now its membership has more than tripled since Zack Polanski became leader. Under the party’s direct-democracy model, policy is voted on only by members who attend one of its two annual conferences, a system some Greens believe risks empowering organised fringe activists who make the effort to travel to the events. Continue reading...
items.body: Exclusive: Concerns in-person vote system is giving bigger say to organised fringe activists who can attend conference Senior Greens are examining ways to revamp the party’s structures to make it more effective and representative now its membership has more than tripled since Zack Polanski became leader. Under the party’s direct-democracy model, policy is voted on only by members who attend one of its two annual conferences, a system some Greens believe risks empowering organised fringe activists who make the effort to travel to the events. items.title: Spotlight falls on Ocado boss Tim Steiner’s £100m in payouts
items.summary: Reports claim replacement being lined up for co-founder amid concern over high pay and company’s struggling share price The boss of Ocado has collected nearly £100m since the online grocery company floated on the stock market in 2010, despite its share price now languishing below its flotation level, analysis has shown. Tim Steiner, a former Goldman Sachs trader who co-founded the British technology company in 2000, is thought to be in discussions over his future after it emerged Ocado had approached at least one potential replacement. Continue reading...

items.title: Voter registration at 64%, four months before elections
items.summary: Words: GroundUp After the registration weekend on 20 and 21 June, there were 28.5-million people on the voters’ roll. That’s about 64% of people in South Africa who are 18 years and older. The percentage has not increased since the last government elections in 2021, when there were 26.2-million people and about 40.6-million eligible voters. There are, however, more people...
